In more recent years this sense of security has passed, physical game copies have been replaced by access codes to software and access can be removed all to easy.
The game studio making this game decided it was no longer profitable and therefore shut down the servers making the game forever unplayable with no option for players that wished to continue to play.

paragraphs.text: Greetings. I'm not often politically active, in fact this is the first time I'm sending a letter of this nature. I am 28 years old and video games have been a rather large part of my upbringing. When i was younger this issue was a lot simpler as we bought and owned a physical copy of the game. It was something we could own and not something any company could come and take away from us just to sell us another updated version of said game. In fact we still have access to many of these games for generations to come. In more recent years this sense of security has passed, physical game copies have been replaced by access codes to software and access can be removed all to easy. In addition many games now require online connectivity in order to play either being a game for multiple players or single players. A game such as this was called "worlds adrift" It was a game i cherished deeply and created a lot of great memories with my friends. The game studio making this game decided it was no longer profitable and therefore shut down the servers making the game forever unplayable with no option for players that wished to continue to play. There were several attempts made by communities of players to host their own servers or try to reverse-engineer the game in order to let players once again play the game but alas as the server code was locked away by the publisher this was not possible even in the case of dedicated individuals wanting to host these servers free of charge with no cost to the publisher. Not to long a go the publisher announced that they would release a new game called "lost skies" that more or less is a revamped version of their past game "worlds adrift" selling it at a premium at steam store. The reason i take to write this letter is that i wish these publishers were held accountable to their end of the deal we make when buying games. An end of life plan should be a bare minimum that they should come up with and as someone who also works in as a software developer i must say that it is nothing but greed and laziness that prevents them from doing what the rest of the software industry already are doing. Let us together protect our buyers rights and stop these massive companies from killing more games in the future. Thank you for your time. - Tobias
